sentences.text: It must have been in April, I suppose, because where she was-- [AUDIO OUT]
OK.
I'm Catheryn Morgan, nee Catarina Ilkovic, born in Vrutky, Czechoslovakia, April 21, '28.
I-L-K-- I'm sorry.
I-L-K-O-V-I-C.
V-R-U-T-K-Y.
It's in northern Slovakia.
V-R-U-T-K-Y.
Vrutky.
Northern Czechoslovakia, right.
My father's name was Eugene.
My mother's name was Elizabeth.
And I had one sister called Eva, who was three years older.
Mother's maiden name was Klein, and she had one sister, Rose.
I grew up in an apartment in Vrutky.
My father had a pharmacy, and we lived in the same building where the pharmacy was.
But my grandparents had a beautiful villa in Vrutky where we spent most of our time, since they had a huge garden.
And so my sister and I and, as a matter of fact, my aunt Rose's son George, we spent all summers right there.
And I-- we stayed there until about 1932, to the best of my recollection, when we moved to Ruzonberg.
It's another town in the same area.
And we left Vrutky.
Ruzonberg would be R-U-Z-O-N-B-E-R-G.
My mother worked with my father in the pharmacy, as a matter of fact, yes.
My mother's parents, and they were what you would call upper-middle class.
They were quite well-to-do.
My grandfather had three stone quarries.
I remember the name of two of them, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] And the third one, I don't know.
And they were quite well-to-do.
And on my mother's side, we were related to Gustav and Rosa Lang, who was the sister of Mr.
Hertz, from the famous Hertz [NON-ENGLISH] family.
And we were the last one to see them before they were-- when they were taken away, in fact.
Yes, very vivid and very beautiful memories, as I said.
In the beautiful villa, with the glorious garden-- they had a Dresden gardener, and to his dismay, we children were always in the flowers.
And they were truly lovely, lovely memories.
That was preschool for me.
And then we moved to-- when I moved to [NON-ENGLISH], I started school there.
My father's parents, I knew only my grandfather.
I never met the grandmother.
He visited once.
He was a very tall, lean gentleman.
And to the best of my recollection, he must have died while I was still in-- we were still in Vrutky.
But I've never visited the-- the place where they lived, and I really wouldn't even know, actually, where.
All told?
Well, I started school in [NON-ENGLISH].
I went to elementary school.
And we lived there until 1939.
From '33 through '39, which would be six years.
And then we moved to a place when things were getting bad.
That was Czechoslovakia.
We fled to Hungary.
And we fled to an area which was once Hungary, and once Czechoslovakia.
It changed hands a couple of times.
The place is called [NON-ENGLISH], and it was near Uzhgorod.
Uzhgorod would be-- well, being that it was Hungary, it would be Ungvar, which would be U-N-G-V-A-R.
And by then I was in high school.
And I went to the Hebrew high school, where under relatively normal condition, we really went to school up until March, 1944, when the German marched into Hungary.
Well, not really for a typical day, but it was very normal.
And as you know, Czechoslovakia was a very democratic country.
It was patterned after the USA, and conditions were very nice.
I never knew anything about anti-Semitism.
I was a excellent student.
And school years were happy ones, and without any incidents that I should, really, remember.
No, we were not.
Not at all.
I knew that we were Jewish, of course.
And my mother would light candle Friday evenings.
But otherwise, we really did not keep a what you call an orthodox home.
Still, of course, I was well-aware that we were Jewish.
And we went to Hebrew classes in school, where we were taught Bible and some [INAUDIBLE].
But that-- that was about the extent of my Jewish background.
I-- that was very important to us.
We were very chauvinistic, and we only spoke Czech, while my parents-- I caught them many times speaking either Hungarian or German, because when they were born, that was belonged to Austro-Hungaria.
But we children, we would never to be caught talking Hungarian.
In fact, I didn't know any until we fled to Hungary.
And from one day to the next, I was sitting in school, and it was totally foreign to me.
And I had to learn it in a big hurry in order to continue school.
Yes, we were very close.
She was three years older, but we were extremely close, and we did a lot of things together.
And of course, I looked up to her as a bigger sister.
And she was beautiful and lady like.
I was a real tomboy and terrible and she was really my example how to be a lady.
Yes, we had a beautiful room, just for the two of us.
As I said, we were quite well-to-do, so we lived under very nice and comfortable conditions, and we had our own room.
I even remember the color.
It was willow green.
And we had our own room.
This was an apartment.
Never lived in a house.
Yes.
I do very well.
It was a beautiful corner apartment, with an entrance hall.
Then there was what they called the salon, which would be today's living room.
Then from my parent's bedroom, our bedroom, kitchen, bath, and a foyer.
I don't remember the address.
I remember the address of my school.
But funny, I don't remember that address, no.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]
Yes.
Ruzonberg was small, but it was a town.
It was not a village.
And it was a town, and we walked to school.
Absolutely.
Yes, there were.
Yes.
Indeed, several of my friends were, and some were not.
And there was a synagogue.
And there was, unfortunately, I really don't know.
[INAUDIBLE].
Certainly not more.
Probably the most would be 20%, I would assume.
Were not Jewish, and we were very close friends.
I know that for Passover, we had the matzah, and that had to be brought in, I remember vividly, from somewhere else, because there were none available in Ruzonberg itself.
So it must have been a small Jewish community.
And the ones that were, we were rather assimilated, as the cliche goes.
I wasn't in that stage of dating, so I wouldn't know anything about that.
Of course, I was much too young.
Yes.
Yes, we remained a very close-knit family, since my grandparents had only two children-- my mother and Aunt Rose.
And we were very close, indeed.
And every Sunday, my grandfather with his chauffeur and a beautiful [? hotmobile ?] car would pick us up, and we would go in the nearby countryside or to a nearby resort.
There we would have all dinner on a lovely terrace.
I have recollections of that.
Me?
Sports.
I skied before I walked, and rode a two-wheel bicycle before I walked, and did all the things that little boys do instead of little girls.
None at all, no.
None whatsoever.
I was never aware that I'm any different than the other kids, except for religion.
We went-- as I said, we attended some-- whatever you call it.
Bible classes, or whatever.
Yeah, well, my father always wanted us to follow in his footsteps and study either chemistry or be a pharmacist.
So we sort of had dreamt of that.
But I was much too young to make any decision to that, since that was way before you would have to decide.
Only that it was 1939, and the [NON-ENGLISH] was occupied by Germans.
And we heard rumors.
And we also heard rumor that some men were taken away to the police station, and they were beaten, but I have never seen it.
And my father-- we always felt very secure, because my father was a high-ranking officer, once upon a time.
And-- and in fact, he had the only pharmacy.
So it was called like a vital occupation.
So somehow, it-- we were not affected by it.
They did, to a certain extent.
And my father, bless his memory, he just refused to believe that it can happen.
They, evidently, heard the rumors.
And in fact, he had many connections in Switzerland, because he was a pharmacist, and through the drug companies.
And they urged him to come, and to flee.
But of course, who would leave under normal condition your everything, and your families, and be uprooted?
And he just never believed that it can happen, in spite of the fact that, towards the end, that I remember hearing rumors-- that people were actually taken away from their homes.
He had an older brother called Sigmund, who was also a pharmacist.
And they had a pharmacy in Nitra.
And they remained in Czechoslovakia.
And we heard that they were taken away.
And we heard some indirect rumors that the situation was quite, quite serious and bad in that area.
But that's the last we heard from them, never again.
They had one daughter, Vera.
I understand she survived the war.
I tried to locate her, but I never could.
About war years?

Not really, except that towards the end, she told me she had a dreadful dream that we were all being herded-- I mean, there was no name of concentration camps-- like to one area, and that we all died.
I'll never forget it.
She told me that, yes.
But we were not aware of any immediate danger, so to speak.
But I do remember, vividly, as I said, that particular--
Well, it changed drastically and rapidly on that fateful day, when the German marched into Hungary, which was, I believe, March the 17th.
I'm not sure.
I know it's in March.
That was 1944.
And like from one day to the next, school stopped.
Life stopped, as such.
And suddenly, we find ourself-- my father was-- and us, we were just asked to leave the apartment and the pharmacy.
And they moved us.
First we lived in, I don't know, somebody else's apartment for a few days.
And a few days later, we were all taken to Uzhgorod, to Ungvar, where-- it wasn't a ghetto, but it was a gathering area in an old brick factory, which was, like, in the open.
And that's where, obviously, they brought in everyone from the neighboring villages and towns.
And that's where we stayed-- and mercifully, I don't remember how long-- under very primitive condition, until we were taken, then, by train on that fateful day to Auschwitz.
How long we stayed there, I have no recollection.
No, no, none.
As a child, I don't remember.
I only remember-- vaguely, I remember that my father told my mother to hide her engaged-- her-- obviously, her wedding band and her engagement ring.
But no personal possession that I recall at all, that I had taken along.
No, this was after school that we found out, and we never returned to school again.
It was abrupt, sudden, and without, so to speak, really any prior warning.
This was where the Hungarians had taken us.
And I will never forgive them, because they were worse than the Germans.
They did it on their own, over-zealously.
And they herded us all in.
My grandmother, who-- I forgot to say.
In the meantime, my grandfather passed away.
And my grandmother lived with us.
So it was my grandmother, my Aunt Rose, my mother and father, and my sister.
And we were all in this little cubicle, which wasn't much privacy, because the whole thing was an open area.
Still, we stuck together.
And the only thing I remember is that one day, my grandmother complained-- may she rest in peace-- that she was hungry.
And we couldn't give her anything, because there was none.
Aunt Rose's son fled.
They were divorced, and his family lived in Rome.
And they took him to Rome.
And he spent his whole war years in Italy, in Rome.
And Aunt Rose stayed with us.
She lived with us.
And she's very special, if I may say, now.
She's very special.
And I'm glad that I have this opportunity to mention, because while my mother gave me life the first time, Aunt Rose, truly the second.
Because I was with her in Auschwitz and afterwards.
And if not for her, I surely would have perished later.
I was emaciated and ill.
And she brought me, literally, back to life.
George.
He's still alive.
They, obviously, must have heard that things are bad.
And he, himself, lived in France.
And he took the son to Italy, to his sisters, who were married to Italians that lived in Rome.
No, I have no recollection.
My-- it was days, maybe weeks, but certainly not months, because by May we were in Auschwitz.
Not that I recall.
None.
Evidently, whatever we had with us.
And eventually, that dwindled down.
No, mercifully, I don't.
The only thing I remember, being taken to the train.
And we were searched.
And it took hours and hours.
And again, the overseer was Hungarian policeman with that unforgettable feather cap they had.
They were herding us, and they were yelling and screaming.
And they said, if you don't give us what you have now, we will shoot you.
And that I remember vividly.
And then, before we were herded, one terrible memory that will always remain with me, they ask my sister and me-- leaving my parents, and they took us to the building.
At the time, we didn't know why.
And we were so naive.
We were so young.
And there was a whole group of them, and they made us undress-- totally naked.
They never harmed us.
They never touched us.
But they made us undress.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then after a while, they send us back to my parents.
And I heard them laugh, of course, and making remarks.
Obviously, what, mercifully I don't know.
We were all together.
We were taken in to this dreadful train, and we were herded into the train, on the way to Auschwitz.
How many days it took, I have no recollection.
Conditions were dreadful.
I remember very little of it.
And all I know is that hum of the train-- ever since then, I detest trains, and I can't look at them.
In fact, when we're out in a station, and there is a-- a-- what do they call the train that are-- not the passenger, the other kind.
The freight trains.
My husband always tells me, look away.
No, we did not.
We did not where we were herded.
The whole family, lots of other people into the cattle car, and off we went.
How many days, I don't know.
And we don't-- we had no idea where we are heading, what we ate, or how we-- mercifully, I don't remember.
The grandmother, my aunt, mother and father, and sister.
Yes.
My mother was barely 42 years old, 43.
Yeah.
No, I don't remember eating anything.
No, neither.
I guess when you are a kid, you don't-- you are not aware of that.
And as far as food, mercifully, it's blocked.
Finally, the train's door opened.
And the first thing we heard, yelling and screaming of inmates who came to the train in striped suits.
We didn't know who they were, but all-- we heard them yelling in all kind of languages, they-- give the children to the old.
Give the children to the old.
Well, being that we didn't have small children, we were one group.
The next thing we know, my father was gone.
We never had a chance to say goodbye.
He disappeared, and the next thing we know is, we were standing in front of the infamous Dr.
Mengele, and my grandmother to the left, my sister, my mother, and myself and Aunt Rose to the right.
And that's the last we saw my grandparents.
And we were herded, then, into some barracks, where for hours and hours, we were standing.
And obviously, by then I remember being thirsty.
There was no water.
And were taken to-- and they told us we are being taken to a shower.
But we never saw a shower, but they started to shave off our hair.
And we were-- they took our clothes, incidentally, and we were thrown some dreadful rags.
And we looked at it-- we couldn't see ourselves.
We had no mirror.
But we could see each other.
And it was a dreadful sight, with the shaven heads.

And then, God knows how many hours, or maybe, a day later, finally, we were taken to the barracks in Auschwitz, in Lager B, where we were still together, the four of us, my mother, Aunt Rose, my sister, and myself.
And that's where life started.
They didn't need us to work.
We were not [INAUDIBLE] anymore.
That was towards the end.
We came in with a transport of gypsies, incidentally.
I understand the poor gypsies were taken immediately to the gas chambers.
And we did not work.
But we had these terrible zahlappells, which are infamous by now.
People seem to realize just what it was, twice a day.
And we felt so bad for our mother, standing.
And I have two things I remember about Auchwitz-- the blazing sun, and the mud.
It was always gray, and muddy, and cold.
And we had no clothes.
And for hours on end, we were standing for the count.
But we were never beaten, and we were never sent to work.
Well, it must have been very early, because it was still dark.
And they herded us out.
I guess that's when you had the facilities for the infamous toilets, which, luckily, I don't remember much of.
And then there were-- all I remember, standing outside in rows of five, to the best of my recollection.
And they counted, and counted, and recounted us.
And whoever couldn't stand, of course, passed out.
And there was no end to it.
And then we were herded back.

And then, I guess, came the meal, which consisted of some kind of a black liquid with bread that the girls put certain-- girls were-- I don't know that they were volunteers, or they were drafted to bring it from the kitchen.
And we were given that.
And we just stayed, until the next zahlappell, which wasn't too long after.
And that went on, day in, day out.
We slept in-- on these wooden bunks.
There were about five of us to each row.
And we tried to keep neat and clean and wash ourselves as much as we could, of course.
But it was getting increasingly difficult.
We started to lose weight, rapidly.
And things got from bad to worse.
And we were looking over with envy.
It was mostly German women who came out there, and they were doing the count.
And they came with their beautiful, freshly-ironed blouses.
And they wore boots.
And to this day, I hate culottes, they all wore.
And I-- and you know, what you do for horses and all that.
The whip, in her hand.
No, no.
Grandmother was gone.
We didn't have her with.
No, we just-- I don't remember, except that we-- she must have felt terrible, for to see her children.
And we felt, in turn, terrible for her being out there.
And each day's selection came while we were on the zahlappell.
Each day was a selection, and each day we were grateful that we are still together.
And that went on until the day that will live in infamy, October 10, 1944, when Mengele came to the barracks, himself.
And that was the final selection.
And while my aunt and I went to the right, my sister and mother to the left.
And that's the last time that I have seen them.
We were taken from there to a railroad station.
We didn't know where we go.
It was dark.
And they told us again, we're going to some showers.
So obviously, we must have gone through some kind of a disinfecting station.
And the next thing I knew, I was again on a train.
And they took us to a place that I cannot find on the map.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH].
We were the so-called able-bodied, my aunt and I.
And there was a relatively small camp, women only.
And we were taken for slave labor.
We were digging ditches, anti-tank ditches.
And they took us out every morning in this bitter cold, with a little rag on our bones.
And we had to-- I had a shovel that I could hardly lift.
And we were, allegedly, doing this work from morning until night, outdoors.
We were back to the barracks, and we stayed there until about, almost January or end of December of 1944.
[NON-ENGLISH], you think it was--
[NON-ENGLISH].
We keep thinking, because it couldn't be too far from Auschwitz.
Only it was a very short train ride, and I cannot find it on the map.
But it was definitely a slave labor camp for women only.
And as I said, there were the anti-tank ditches.
By then, of course, rumors were-- and even we heard it-- that the Russians are approaching.
So one day, they herded us into a row.
And that's when the infamous death march to Bergen-Belsen started.
We-- yes, we were-- we were aware.
And we also heard-- I mean, there were some prisoners that came around doing some odd duties.
I don't know who they were.
They were political prisoner, men.
And they were telling us that it's [GERMAN], which means, of course, that it's a camp where we are for one purpose only.
And that sooner or later, you see that smoke, they would point out.
That's where you're going to wind up yourself.
And they are the ones who told us that the children were thrown alive into these pits.
And older people were taken to the-- what they called, euphemistically, a shower, which was, of course, the gas chambers.
So we found out from them, otherwise we wouldn't have known.
We-- and the smell, of course.
Then we realized the dreadful smell, and we knew those were the burned bodies that we smelled.
No, we did-- we had no knowledge where.
We tried to think that they weren't taken, indeed, right away to the gas chambers.
Of course, absolutely.
Even afterwards.
After the war, I thought I'd find my family.
I thought they would manage to escape.
No, not at all-- except, of course, we were thin.
But we all were.
My mother was relatively young.
The mistake was, they were told us then, that automatically he sent one to the left, one to the right.
And we went in succession.
We should have not.
Perhaps we would have been together.
But unfortunately, he just sort of automatically pushed right and left, and--
None.
None whatsoever.
The following day was my sister's birthday, October the 11th.
And I remember that we all saved some bread, so we can [CRIES] give it to her.
But it never came to it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that was the last time that I was in Auschwitz.
OK.
